input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: From a deontological perspective, the question of whether to turn the trolley is not about the consequences, but about the moral duty and principles involved. It is wrong to actively intervene and cause the death of an innocent person, regardless of the potential outcome. The moral rule here is that one should not kill an innocent person, even if it means saving five others. The ends do not justify the means. Therefore, I would argue that one should not turn the trolley.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, but I must respectfully disagree. From a utilitarian standpoint, the moral worth of an action is determined by its outcome. In this scenario, the outcome of not turning the trolley is the death of five people, while the outcome of turning the trolley is the death of one. The action that leads to the least amount of harm, in this case, turning the trolley, is the morally correct choice. It's not about the intent, but about minimizing the total harm.

[Deontologist]: I see your point, but I must emphasize that deontological ethics does not reduce morality to a simple calculation of outcomes. There are certain actions that are inherently wrong, regardless of the consequences. In this case, actively choosing to divert the trolley to kill one person violates the moral principle that it is wrong to use a person as a means to an end. The fact that five lives might be saved does not make it morally permissible to sacrifice one individual. The duty not to kill an innocent person is a categorical imperative that must be upheld, even in the face of seemingly dire consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your emphasis on moral principles, but I must challenge the idea that certain actions are inherently wrong regardless of consequences. If we strictly adhere to the principle that it's wrong to use a person as a means to an end, we might end up with outcomes that are morally worse. For instance, what if the one person on the spur is a known criminal, while the five on the main track are innocent children? Would the principle still hold, or would the consequences start to matter more? Utilitarianism allows for flexibility in such scenarios, always aiming to minimize harm and maximize benefit.

[Deontologist]: I understand your argument, but I must clarify that deontological ethics does not advocate for rigid adherence to rules without consideration for the context. The principle that it is wrong to use a person as a means to an end applies to all individuals, regardless of their moral character. In your example, even if the one person is a known criminal, it is still morally impermissible to actively choose to kill them to save five innocent children. The moral worth of an action is not determined by the identity of the victim but by the inherent wrongness of the act itself. Utilitarianism may allow for flexibility, but it risks justifying actions that are fundamentally unjust or immoral in pursuit of a greater good. The duty to respect the inherent worth and dignity of every individual must always be upheld.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about the inherent worth and dignity of every individual, but I must ask: what about the inherent worth and dignity of the five people who would die if we don't act? Is their value less because they are not directly in our line of sight? Utilitarianism seeks to balance the scales, to minimize the total harm. It's not about justifying unjust actions, but about making the most morally responsible choice in a given situation. If we can save five lives at the cost of one, isn't that the most responsible thing to do?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern for the five individuals, but the deontological perspective does not measure the value of lives in a utilitarian calculus. Each person has inherent worth and dignity, and it is not for us to decide whose life is more valuable. The act of diverting the trolley to kill one person is an active choice to end a life, which is morally impermissible. It is not about the number of lives saved, but about the moral duty to refrain from actively causing harm. The utilitarian approach may seem practical, but it risks reducing individuals to mere numbers in a cost-benefit analysis. The moral principle here is clear: it is wrong to actively choose to kill an innocent person, regardless of the consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I hear your emphasis on moral duty and the inherent worth of each individual. However, I must press further: what if the one person on the spur is a tyrant, whose continued existence would lead to the deaths of thousands? Would the principle still hold, or would the consequences start to outweigh the moral duty? Utilitarianism acknowledges the complexity of such scenarios and seeks to navigate them in a way that minimizes harm. It's not about reducing individuals to numbers, but about making the most morally responsible choice given the available options.

[Deontologist]: Even in the case of a tyrant, the deontological perspective maintains that it is wrong to actively choose to kill an individual, regardless of the potential future consequences. The moral principle here is about the inherent wrongness of the act itself, not the identity or actions of the victim. While the utilitarian approach may seem to offer a practical solution, it risks justifying actions that are fundamentally unjust or immoral. The duty to refrain from actively causing harm is a categorical imperative that must be upheld, even in the face of complex scenarios. The ends do not justify the means, and the moral worth of an action is not determined by its outcomes but by its adherence to moral principles.

[Utilitarian]: I see that we are at an impasse, as our fundamental ethical frameworks lead us to different conclusions. I appreciate the depth of our discussion and the clarity with which you've presented your perspective. While I still believe that minimizing harm is the most morally responsible approach, I respect your commitment to moral principles. Perhaps we can agree that this dilemma highlights the complexity of ethical decision-making and the importance of considering multiple perspectives.
